context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Migration and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How is migration related to development? To what extent are remittances the "new development mantra"? What role can states play in promoting the migration-development nexus? How can a scalar approach help us in identifying the development impacts of transnational migration? How does a transnational optic help us understand and uncover the links between development and migration?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection on migration and development. I’m kinda stuck on how to connect remittances, the state’s role, and the scalar/transnational stuff without it sounding random.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've already identified the key pieces — that's a useful start. Before we dig in, what do you think is the central argument connecting all of those pieces? In other words, if you had to state in one sentence what migration has to do with development, what would you say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and has identified the main components of the prompt. Rather than scaffolding all the pieces for them, I should ask them to articulate their current thinking first, so I can understand where they actually are and guide from there using Socratic questioning. This keeps the discovery process student-led.

exchanges.student: Uhh maybe that migration affects development bc ppl send money home and also bring back ideas/networks? But idk how to make that broad enough for the whole prompt.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid starting point — you're touching on both financial and non-financial flows, which is important. Now, your sentence focuses on what migrants do. But who else shapes whether those flows actually lead to development? Think about the actors involved beyond the migrants themselves.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a decent intuition about remittances and social remittances/networks. I should affirm the direction without validating it as complete, and push them to think about what's missing — specifically the state's role and the scalar/transnational dimensions. A single probing question will do.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing the state, families, employers, and maybe intl orgs too? I’m not fully sure which ones matter most for this prompt tho.
exchanges.tutor: Good list. The prompt specifically singles out the state though — why do you think that is? What can the state do that families, employers, or NGOs can't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is listing actors correctly but is unsure of prioritization. The prompt specifically asks about the state's role, so I should redirect focus there without giving away what that role is. A targeted question will help them think through what's unique about the state as an actor compared to others.

exchanges.student: Maybe bc the state makes the rules? Like visas, labor policy, banking stuff, and public investment, so it can shape whether migration helps or hurts development.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — the state as rule-maker is a key idea here. Now, migration happens across borders, involving multiple states and multiple scales (local, national, global). How does that complexity change how we analyze its development impacts? What does thinking 'scalar' or 'transnational' add that a single-country view would miss?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is arriving at the 'rule-maker' concept from the syllabus, which is central to the course's framing of the state. I should affirm this and now push them toward the scalar/transnational dimensions of the prompt, which they haven't addressed yet.

exchanges.student: I think it shows impacts aren’t just in one country — like a village can benefit from remittances while the country loses workers, or migrants gain rights in one place and families depend on them somewhere else. I’m still fuzzy on the diff between scalar and transnational tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Good examples. Let's unpack the terms themselves — what does 'scale' suggest to you, and what does 'transnational' suggest? They sound similar but emphasize different things. What's the difference between thinking in levels versus thinking across borders?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good intuitions about multi-level impacts but is genuinely confused about the distinction between scalar and transnational approaches. This is a conceptual point worth clarifying through questioning rather than explaining directly. I'll ask them to think about what each word itself implies to help them arrive at the distinction.

exchanges.student: Scale feels like levels, like household/local/national/global. Transnational feels more like the actual cross-border links and networks between those levels, I think?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong distinction — you've got it. You now have the key pieces: the migration-development link, remittances and their limits, the state as rule-maker, and the scalar vs. transnational lenses. Try drafting a short thesis statement or outline that ties these together. What's the central argument your reflection will make?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly distinguished the two concepts. I should affirm this clearly and now push them toward integration — they have all the building blocks now (remittances, state role, scalar, transnational) and should start thinking about how to structure their argument cohesively. We've had several exchanges building the conceptual foundation, so it's time to push toward a written attempt.

exchanges.student: Maybe my thesis is that migration shapes development through money, social ties, and political/economic links, but remittances alone are overrated, and states plus scalar/transnational analysis show the effects are uneven. Does that sound too generic?
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is right — it reads more like a summary of topics than an argument. Ask yourself: what is the specific claim you're making that someone could disagree with? What's the 'so what' that makes your reflection worth reading?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is self-critical in a productive way — they sense their thesis is too broad. Rather than telling them how to sharpen it, I should ask them to identify what's specifically arguable or debatable in their statement, pushing them toward a more precise claim.

exchanges.student: Maybe: remittances get treated like a fix for underdevelopment, but that hides how states offload responsibility onto migrants, and a scalar/transnational view shows migration creates uneven gains and losses across places.
exchanges.tutor: That's much sharper — there's a real argument there with stakes. Now, how would you organize the reflection around that thesis? What would each section need to do to build toward that conclusion?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a notably stronger thesis — it has a clear arguable claim, a critique, and a methodological payoff. I should affirm this progress clearly and now push the student toward thinking about how to structure the reflection around this thesis, since we're approaching the point where they should be writing.

exchanges.student: I think intro + thesis, then a para on migration/development beyond remittances, one on why remittances are the “new mantra” but limited, one on state roles, and one on scalar vs transnational lens. Ngl I’m not sure if scalar and transnational should be split or combined.
exchanges.tutor: Good structure overall. Think about your thesis — scalar shows uneven gains across levels, transnational shows cross-border links. Do those two ideas serve different functions in your argument, or are they making the same point? That should tell you whether they need separate space.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid structure and is asking a genuine organizational question about scalar vs. transnational. Rather than answering directly, I should ask them to think about what each lens contributes to their specific thesis argument — that will help them decide whether to split or combine organically.

exchanges.student: Yeah, diff functions I think — scalar = where impacts show up, transnational = how they’re produced through cross-border ties. So maybe separate but linked paras makes more sense.
exchanges.tutor: That distinction holds up well. You've got a strong thesis and a clear structure — at this point, the best next step is to start writing. Come back with a draft or a section you're unsure about and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked through all the major conceptual pieces and has a clear structure. We've had about 9 exchanges and they have solid building blocks. It's time to push them to start writing rather than continuing to plan. I should affirm their reasoning and redirect them to the actual draft.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic session across all 10 exchanges. Key ideas — the migration-development link, remittances as a limited 'mantra,' the state as rule-maker, and the scalar vs. transnational distinction — were all elicited from the student through targeted questioning rather than being given outright. The tutor consistently built on student responses, maintained a supportive tone, kept responses concise, and ended by directing the student to begin drafting independently.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under any criterion. The tutor never provided submission-ready content or rewrote student text; every substantive idea was drawn out through questioning (1.1). The tutor acknowledged and built on each student contribution sequentially, and used diagnostic questions before advancing (1.2). Method-level feedback was given when the student's thesis was too generic (Turn 7), guiding the student toward a sharper argument (1.3). There was no repetition without progress — each turn advanced the conversation (2.1). The tutor stayed anchored to the assignment throughout with no off-topic drift (2.2). Responses were consistently brief and well-targeted (3.1), and tone was warm, encouraging, and formative throughout with no grading language (3.2).
